title: FINS3666 2025 T1 Assessment 1 Activity 1 Technical Specifications
body:
Technical Specifications: Assessment 1, Activity 1

1. Data Processing

The data associated with this in class exercise are based on observations on the single day of 15-Jan-2025 for the stock Iress Ltd which is traded on both the ASX (IRE.AX) and on Cboe Australia (IRE.CHA). The data are provided in two (2) files: 1. Trades IRE.X 20250115 Stocks Trades.csv 2. Orders IRE.X 20250115 Market Depth Legacy x 10.csv.

The data processing necessary for the exercises in this assessment are relatively basic, they involve: • Assigning numerical values to the time field, then restricting the analysis to the period known as ‘Continuous Trading’, Continuous Trading starts after the Opening Auction is finished, which is generally 10:10:00 and before the Closing Auction is started, which is 16:00:00 • Aligning the observations of Orders with Trades. For the given date of 15-Jan-2025 we observe 6,143 Trades and a maximum of 31,368 Order observations. However to construct our statistical analysis we need to match Trades & Orders on a one-to-one basis, which we do by determining the most recent Order before each Trade.

Restricting our input data to the period of Continuous Trading removes most issues with respect to missing values, outliers, anomalies and other complications.

Further processing of the data to improve our analysis could include study of the ‘Qualifier’ data field to take into account features such as on-market vs. off-market trades, lit vs. hidden trades, different types of ‘Crossings’, e.g. special size, NBBO etc. CRICOS Provider Code 00098G  2. Basic Microstructure Analysis

The microstructure measures we will calculate come from data associated with either Orders, Trades or a combination of Orders & Trades.

2.1. Microstructure Basics – Trades

Trade Feed metrics are relatively straight forward. Within the file IRE.X 20250115 Stocks Trades.xlsb we use Pivot Tables to observe the following metrics.

2.1.1. Trade Volume

Definition: The trade volume is the cumulative amount of each trade size up to the indicated time.

𝒎

𝑽𝒕 = # 𝑵𝑻𝒕 𝑁&' = trade size / volume at time t 𝑉& = cumulative trade size / volume up till time t

2.1.2. Trade Count

Definition: The trade count is the cumulative number of trades up to the indicated time.

𝒎

𝑪𝒕 = # 𝟏{𝑵𝑻𝒕 } 𝑁&' = trade size / volume at time t 𝐶&' = trade count at time t

2.1.3. Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP)

Definition: The average price of a security weighted by trade volume over a given time period is commonly referred to as the Volume Weighted Average Price (VWAP). VWAP is a

benchmark to gauge how well trades are executed relative to the average market price, and is given by the formula:

∑𝒎 𝑻 𝑻 𝒕$𝟏(𝒑𝒕 × 𝑵𝒕 ) ∑𝒎 𝑻 𝑻 𝒕$𝟏(𝒑𝒕 × 𝑵𝒕 ) 𝒑𝑽𝒕 = = ∑𝒎 𝑻 𝒕$𝟏 𝑵𝒕 𝑽𝒕 𝑝&) = volume weighted average price (VWAP) at time t 𝑝&' = traded / executed price at time t 𝑁&' = trade size / volume at time t 𝑉& = cumulative trade size / volume up till time t

Example: • Trade 1: Price = $100, Volume = 500 • Trade 2: Price = $101, Volume = 300 • Trade 3: Price = $99, Volume = 200 ($,--×/--0$,-,×1--0$22×3--) • Therefore VWAP is given by 𝑝&) = (/--01--03--) = $100.10

Use case: Helps traders evaluate trade execution and spot buying or selling pressure.

It is also instructive to observe Trade Feed metrics using charts as per IRE.X 20250115 Stocks Trades.xlsb of: • Traded Prices x Volume • Traded Prices x VWAP x Time • Traded Prices x Trade Count x Time • Traded Prices x Bid Prices x Ask Prices x Time.

The last chart mentioned above provides an indication of where Trades are occurring relative to Orders. Trades occurring at Bid Prices often indicate that a Market Order has been used to Sell stock and hence downward pressure on prices. The converse is when Trades execute at Ask Prices, generally indicating that a Market Order has been used to Buy stock and hence upward pressure on prices. This is an example of how Trade Feed & Order Book information can be combined to generate meaningful insights, further examples are given in the next section below.

2.2. Microstructure Basics – Orders

Order Book metrics involve more analysis and data manipulation than Trade Feed metrics. More specifically, the following Order Book metrics, as calculated within the file IRE.X 20250115 Market Depth Legacy x 10.xlsb, are typically considered the most important for understanding market dynamics.

2.2.1. Bid-Ask Spread

Definition: The difference between the best (highest) bid price and the best (lowest) ask price. It reflects the cost of trading immediately and is a measure of market liquidity:

𝒔𝑩𝑨 𝑨 𝑩 𝒊 ($) = 𝒑𝒊 − 𝒑𝒊 𝑠89: ($) = bid ask spread at the i-th order book level in dollars

Example: if the best ask price is $100.10 and the best bid price is $100.00, then 𝑠,9: ($) = $100.10 − $100.00 = $0.10. The bid ask spread can also be calculated in basis points as follows:

𝟐 × >𝒑𝑨𝒊 − 𝒑𝑩 𝒊 ? 𝒔𝑩𝑨 𝒊 ($) 𝒔𝑩𝑨 𝒊 (𝒃𝒑𝒔) = 𝟏𝟎, 𝟎𝟎𝟎 × 𝑨 𝑩 = 𝟏𝟎, 𝟎𝟎𝟎 × 𝒑𝒊 + 𝒑𝒊 𝒑𝑴 𝒊 𝑠89: (𝑏𝑝𝑠) = bid ask spread at the i-th order book level in basis points 𝑝8< = simple mid price at the i-th order book level

2.2.2. Market Depth

Definition: The total volume of buy and sell orders at various price levels. It shows the supply and demand at different prices and can be expressed using the following set of formulae:

𝒏

𝑫𝑩 𝒊 = # 𝑵𝒊 𝑩

𝒏

𝑫𝑨𝒊 = # 𝑵𝑨𝒊 𝒏

𝑫𝒊 = 𝑫𝑩 𝑨 𝑩 𝑨 𝒊 + 𝑫𝒊 = #>𝑵𝒊 + 𝑵𝒊 ?

𝐷8: = cumulative ask depth at the i-th order book level 𝐷89 = cumulative bid depth at the i-th order book level 𝐷8 = cumulative order book depth at the i-th level

Example: for the top three (3) bid levels: • $99.50: 200 shares • $99.40: 300 shares • $99.30: 500 shares • Then 𝐷19 = 200 + 300 + 500 = 1,000 shares.

It will also be useful to know the available liquidity at each order book level such that:

𝒏

𝑫𝒊 = # 𝐦𝐢𝐧>𝑵𝑩 𝑨 𝑨 𝑩 𝒊 , 𝑵𝒊 ? ≠ 𝐦𝐢𝐧>𝑫𝒊 , 𝑫𝒊 ? 𝑨,𝑩 𝑨,𝑩

𝐷8: = cumulative ask depth at the i-th order book level 𝐷89 = cumulative bid depth at the i-th order book level 𝐷8 = cumulative minimum order book depth at the i-th level

2.2.3. Order Imbalance

Definition: The difference between the total buy volume and the total sell volume at the best bid and best ask prices. It provides insight into potential price direction:

𝑰(𝑵)𝒊 = 𝑵𝑩 𝑨 𝒊 − 𝑵𝒊 𝐼(𝑁)8 = order imbalance at the i-th level in number of shares

Example: • Total Buy Volume at $100.00 (Best Bid): 500 shares • Total Sell Volume at $100.10 (Best Ask): 600 shares • Then 𝐼(𝑁), = 500 − 600 = −100 shares.

Order Imbalance is frequently calculated in percentage terms using the following formula:

𝑵𝑩 𝑨 𝒊 − 𝑵𝒊 𝑰(𝑵)𝒊 𝑰(%)𝒊 = 𝑩 𝑨 = 𝑩 𝑵𝒊 + 𝑵𝒊 𝑵𝒊 + 𝑵𝑨𝒊 𝐼(𝑁)8 = order imbalance at the i-th level in number of shares 𝐼(%)8 = order imbalance at the i-th level in percentage terms

A negative order imbalance indicates more selling pressure.

2.3. Microstructure Basics – Mid Prices

Mid Prices are often the starting point in pricing models used by both Proprietary Traders and Market Makers. Below are four (4) different methods to determine the Mid Price based on Order Book data. This includes the simple "best bid and best ask" method using Top of Book data. However, the Mid-Price can also be calculated using Level 2 data (market depth)

utilising multiple levels of bid and ask prices with respective volumes. We provide three (3) ways to incorporate more levels to calculate a more representative Mid Price.

2.3.1. Simple Mid Price

This is the simplest method, using only the best (highest) bid and best (lowest) ask prices:

𝒑𝑨𝒊 + 𝒑𝑩 𝒊 𝒑𝑴 𝒊 = 𝟐 𝑝8< = simple mid price at the i-th order book level

Example: • Best Bid Price = $100.00 • Best Ask Price = $100.10 • Then 𝑝,< = (100.00 + 100.10)⁄2 = $100.05

2.3.2. Volume Weighted Mid Price

This method weights bid and ask prices by their respective bid and ask volumes at multiple levels:

∑𝒏𝒊$𝟏>𝒑𝑩 𝑩 𝑨 𝑨 𝒊 × 𝑵𝒊 + 𝒑𝒊 × 𝑵𝒊 ? ∑𝒏𝒊$𝟏>𝒑𝑩 𝑩 𝑨 𝑨 𝒊 × 𝑵𝒊 + 𝒑𝒊 × 𝑵𝒊 ? 𝒑𝑴𝑽 𝒊 = = ∑𝒏𝒊$𝟏(𝑵𝑩 𝑨 𝒊 + 𝑵𝒊 ) 𝑫𝒊 𝑝8<) = volume weighted mid price at the i-th order book level 𝐷8 = cumulative order book depth at the i-th level

Example (5 levels): Level (𝒊) Bid Size >𝑵𝑩 𝑩 𝑨 𝑨 𝒊 ? Bid Price >𝒑𝒊 ? Ask Price >𝒑𝒊 ? Ask Size >𝑵𝒊 ? 1 200 $100.00 $100.10 150 2 300 $99.90 $100.20 200 3 250 $99.80 $100.30 300 4 100 $99.70 $100.40 150 5 150 $99.70 $100.50 250

99,830 + 105,330 𝑝8<) = = $100.08 (200 + 300 + 250 + 100 + 150 + 150 + 200 + 300 + 150 + 250)

2.3.3. Spread Crossing Volume Weighted Mid Price

If we assume that all limit orders are converted to market orders, we can use a more realistic method to calculate the Mid Price. This method assumes that a seller will hit the bid price to sell, while a buyer will lift the ask price to buy. The effective Mid Price under this assumption is adjusted based on available liquidity across the order book.

∑𝒏𝒊$𝟏>𝒑𝑩 𝑨 𝑨 𝑩 𝒊 × 𝑵𝒊 + 𝒑𝒊 × 𝑵𝒊 ? ∑𝒏𝒊$𝟏>𝒑𝑩 𝑨 𝑨 𝑩 𝒊 × 𝑵𝒊 + 𝒑𝒊 × 𝑵𝒊 ? 𝒑𝑴𝑪 𝒊 = = ∑𝒏𝒊$𝟏(𝑵𝑩 𝑨 𝒊 + 𝑵𝒊 ) 𝑫𝒊 𝑝8<@ = spread crossing volume weighted mid price at the i-th order book level 𝐷8 = cumulative order book depth at the i-th level

Example (5 levels): Level (𝒊) Bid Size >𝑵𝑩 𝑩 𝑨 𝑨 𝒊 ? Bid Price >𝒑𝒊 ? Ask Price >𝒑𝒊 ? Ask Size >𝑵𝒊 ? 1 200 $100.00 $100.10 150 2 300 $99.90 $100.20 200 3 250 $99.80 $100.30 300

4 100 $99.70 $100.40 150 5 150 $99.70 $100.50 250

104,775 + 100,270 𝑝8<@ = = $100.02 (200 + 300 + 250 + 100 + 150 + 150 + 200 + 300 + 150 + 250)

This method provides a more realistic Mid Price by simulating the market dynamics of crossing the spread. It reflects the price you would effectively receive if all orders were to become market orders. In our example, this method yielded a Transacted Mid-Price of 100.02, compared to the simpler Mid Price of 100.05 calculated using just the best bid and ask prices.

2.3.4. Minimum Depth Weighted Mid Price

This method calculates the mid-price using only the weighted price difference between bid and ask sides. It provides a better balance when there are large imbalances between buy and sell orders at different levels:

∑𝒏𝒊$𝟏>𝒑𝑩 𝑨 𝑩 𝑨 𝒊 + 𝒑𝒊 ? × 𝐦𝐢𝐧>𝑵𝒊 , 𝑵𝒊 ? 𝑨,𝑩 𝒑𝑴𝑴 𝒊 = 𝟐 × ∑𝒏𝒊$𝟏 𝐦𝐢𝐧(𝑵𝑩 𝑨 𝒊 , 𝑵𝒊 ) 𝑨,𝑩

𝑝8<< = minimum depth weighted mid price at the i-th order book level

This method focuses only on the "matching" orders at each level, reflecting the price at which trades are most likely to occur. That is, the Minimum Depth Weighted Mid Price refines the calculation by weighting price levels based on available liquidity at each level. Unlike the simple mid-price, which only considers the top of the book, this method gives a more accurate representation of the market.

Example (5 levels):

Lvl (𝒊) Bid Size >𝑵𝑩 𝒊 ? Bid Price >𝒑𝑩 𝒊 ? Ask Price >𝒑𝑨𝒊 ? Ask Size >𝑵𝑨𝒊 ? Min Size 𝐦𝐢𝐧>𝑵𝑩 𝑨 𝒊 , 𝑵𝒊 ? 𝑨,𝑩

1 200 $100.00 $100.10 150 150 2 300 $99.90 $100.20 200 200 3 250 $99.80 $100.30 300 250 4 100 $99.70 $100.40 150 100 5 150 $99.70 $100.50 250 150

First, we take the minimum of the bid and ask volumes at each price level. Then we can compute the Minimum Depth Weighted Mid Price using the above formula to obtain:

100.05 × 150 + 100.05 × 200 + 100.05 × 250 + 100.05 × 100 + 100.05 × 150 𝑝8<< = = $100.05 (150 + 200 + 250 + 100 + 150)

Interestingly, in this example, the Minimum Depth Weighted Mid Price is the same as the Simple Mid-Price. This is due to the symmetry of the simple Mid Price. However, in markets where there are significant bid ask volume imbalances, this method provides a more realistic measure of market equilibrium by considering the actual available liquidity.

2.3.5. Mid Price Methodology Summary • The Simple Mid-Price is the quickest method and can be accurate in balanced markets but it does not reflect volume depth or order book structure • The Volume Weighted Mid Price tends to shift lower or higher based on where most of the volume is concentrated, which may better reflect the liquidity weighted execution cost • The Spread Crossing Volume Weighted Mid Price is particularly useful for understanding market impact and execution costs for larger orders • The Minimum Depth Weighted Mid Price is useful when there are significant order imbalances by factoring in the liquidity available at different price levels.

The method chosen to determine the Mid Price depends on how much market depth you want to incorporate and the level of precision you require for your analysis.

3. Statistical Analysis

As a means to combine our work on data processing, microstructure analysis with statistical analysis we wish to test the accuracy of each Mid Price methodology to predict Traded Prices. The starting point is to match Trades with Orders observed in the market immediately prior. The best Mid Price calculation method will depend on your evaluation metrics, market conditions, and trading strategy. Using statistical methods discussed below, such as Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Squared Error (MSE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), Correlation and Regression analysis we can rigorously test and compare each Mid Price method to determine which one is the most accurate predictor of future Trades.

3.1. Mean Absolute Error (MAE) The MAE measures the average absolute difference between predicted mid prices and actual traded prices:

𝒏 𝟏 𝜺 𝑴𝑨 = #W𝒑𝑭𝒕 − 𝒑𝑻𝒕 W 𝒏 𝜀 <: = mean absolute error 𝑝&' = traded / executed price at time t • A lower MAE indicates better prediction accuracy • MAE is easy to interpret and directly shows the average prediction error.

3.2. Mean Squared Error (MSE) The MSE measures the average squared difference between predicted mid prices and actual traded prices, giving more weight to larger errors:

𝒏 𝟏 𝜺 𝑴𝑺 = #(𝒑𝑭𝒕 − 𝒑𝑻𝒕 )𝟐 𝒏 𝜀 <E = mean square error 𝑝8' = traded / executed price at time t

• A lower MSE indicates better predictive accuracy • MSE penalises larger errors more than MAE, making it useful when large deviations are particularly undesirable.

3.3. Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) The RMSE converts the error back to the same units as the prices:

𝒏 𝟏 𝜺 𝑹𝑴𝑺 = Y𝜺𝑴𝑺 = Z #(𝒑𝑭𝒕 − 𝒑𝑻𝒕 )𝟐 𝒏

𝜀 G<E = square root of the mean square error • RMSE is sensitive to large errors and useful for interpreting typical prediction deviations.

3.4. Tracking Error (TE) Tracking Error (TE) is a statistic used in many aspects of financial markets. In our case, TE is essentially the standard deviation between the predict mid price and the traded / executed price. For the general case we define:

𝜺𝒕 = 𝒑𝑭𝒕 − 𝒑𝑻𝒕∗ 𝜀& = difference between the forecast mid price at time t and the traded price at time t*.

In this case, the most common definition for TE is given as the sample standard deviation of these differences, and hence:

𝒏 𝑻 𝟏 𝜺 =Z #(𝜺𝒕 − 𝜺[)𝟐 𝒏−𝟏

𝜀 ' = tracking error 𝜀& = difference between the forecast mid price at time t and the traded price at time t* 𝜀̅ = average difference between all of the forecast mid prices and the traded prices

In most uses of TE it Is assumed that 𝜀̅ = 0. Under this assumption, we see that TE is related to the RSME as follows:

𝒏 𝒏 𝟏 𝑻 𝜺 =] × 𝜺𝑹𝑴𝑺 = Z #(𝒑𝑭𝒕 − 𝒑𝑻𝒕 )𝟐 𝒏−𝟏 𝒏−𝟏

𝜀 ' = tracking error 𝜀 G<E = square root of the mean square error 𝑝&' = traded / executed price at time t • The TE uses the denominator (𝒏 − 𝟏) to correct for bias in a sample estimate of standard deviation versus the RSME which uses the denominator 𝒏, because it uses the population mean.

3.5. Correlation Coefficient (R) The correlation coefficient measures the linear relationship between the predicted mid prices and actual traded prices:

𝝈𝑷,𝑻 𝒕 𝝆𝑷,𝑻 𝒕 = 𝝈𝒕 × 𝝈𝑻𝒕 𝑷

𝜌&B,' =correlation between the predicted mid prices and the actual traded prices at time t 𝜎&B,' = covariance between the predicted mid prices and the actual traded prices at time t 𝜎&B = volatility of the predicted mid prices at time t 𝜎&' = volatility of traded / executed prices at time t • Correlation ranges from negative one (-1) to positive one (+1) such that: o +1: Perfect positive correlation o 0: No correlation o -1: Perfect negative correlation

3.6. Regression Analysis

Lastly, we consider the implementation of a Linear Regression model as a tool to predict Traded Price (dependent variable) based only on Mid Prices (independent variable) as inputs.

A linear regression can be used between actual traded prices on predicted mid prices to see how well the mid prices explain the future trades.

𝒑𝑻𝒕 = 𝜷𝟎 + 𝜷𝟏 × 𝒑𝑷𝒕 + 𝜺𝒕 𝑝&' = original traded / executed price at time t 𝛽- = is the intercept coefficient or constant term 𝛽, = is the slope coefficient that indicates the strength and direction of the relationship between the dependent & independent variables 𝜀& = error at time t • The coefficient of determination (R²) measures the proportion of variance in actual trade prices explained by the predicted mid prices • A higher R² indicates better predictive accuracy.

Within the file IRE.X 20250115 Market Depth Legacy x 10.xlsb we use such an approach based on the following assumptions: • Opening Auction data effectively ends at 10:04:52.739 and hence the first Order / Trade data combination is based on the transaction at 10:06:25.094 • 100 sets of Order / Trade data combinations represents a good sample size to predict Traded Prices • A rolling window is implemented until the final transaction prior to the start of the Closing Auction at 15:59:59.559 • All standard regression analysis assumptions are met, i.e. linearity between predictors and outcome, independence of errors, homoscedasticity, normal distribution of errors.

Students should keep in mind that this is a university assessment exercise. It should be noted that there are many ways to improve the analysis presented here to predict Traded Prices in a real world environment. A few non exhaustive suggestions include: • More advanced data processing using knowledge of market structure, e.g. analysis of trade qualifiers such as treatment of off market transactions • Optimisation of our model assumptions, e.g. exponential weighting of historical data, use of dynamic historical data windows to reflect both time and trade / order information • More advanced prediction models and methods, e.g. multivariate linear regression, recursive least squares regression, machine learning (ML), artificial intelligence (AI).

Appendix A: Difference Between Level 1, Level 2 & Level 3 Data

In financial markets, Level 1, Level 2, and Level 3 data provide progressively more detailed information about the stock’s current price and order flow. Here’s how they differ with simple examples.

A.1: Level 1 Data

Provides basic price and volume information: • Current bid price: $99.50 • Current ask price: $100.00 • Last traded price: $99.75 • Volume: 1,000,000 shares traded today

Use case: Useful for general investors or traders to see basic buy/sell prices and the latest trade information.

A.2: Level 2 Data (Market Depth)

Displays price quotes from multiple market participants at various price levels • Buy Orders (Bids): o $99.50, 200 shares o $99.40, 300 shares o $99.30, 500 shares • Sell Orders (Asks): o $100.00, 150 shares o $100.10, 400 shares o $100.20, 300 shares

Use case: Traders use Level 2 to see the depth of the market (supply and demand) and get insight into price movement. It helps identify liquidity and potential resistance or support levels.

A.3: Level 3 Data

Shows everything in Level 2 plus the ability to see who is placing orders and potentially modify or cancel orders. Only market makers, brokers, and institutions generally have access to this information: • Market Maker A:** Bid $99.50, 200 shares • Market Maker B:** Ask $100.00, 150 shares • Market Maker C:** Bid $99.40, 300 shares

Use case: Level 3 is used by brokers and market makers to manage their trades and liquidity and to facilitate more informed order execution.

A.4: Summary • Level 1: Basic pricing and volume information • Level 2: Full order book with price levels and quantities • Level 3: Identifies market participants and their order details.
